author: @tanalafauzan
text: Patent and copyrights is actually just legalized monopoly. That's why it never really worked unless with power. It's against the nature of knowledge. That knowledge belongs to anyone who understands it. One can charge people for teaching them how to swim, but he can't charge people swimming just because he's the first who learned how to swim. The solution is to pay teachers a worthy salary. And just delete this unnatural law of copyrights.

author: @ancapftw9113
text:
Loved the video.

I keep running into other Ancaps that look at the broken patent system and say "we just shouldn't have one".  Essentially they think that because ideas can be copied so easily, that it is impossible to own one.  I'm been hoping for some kind of idea of how to fix the system so that people can still own an idea and profit from putting in the time, energy, and resources to develop it  but not create a vacuum which stops the development of ideas.  I've tried explaining that you only own what you contribute to a concept, but people tend to find that hard to believe.

I think this could be such a system, though.

author: @3D6Space
text: Thanks Robert! You are a great educator and very fair in your outlook. I too think most people are good and it's nice to hear someone else speak my thoughts so succinctly.. open source is the way to go... -t

author: @samueltanh
text: Agree with your point of view. I am an American, (Dipl.-Ing.) Architect, Products Designer. All my life I was protective to my ideas since I am aware that I dealt with emerging countries. The anti dote is to earn reasonable (low), but in volume. Now, I start working together with Indonesian Micro Finance/Non Profit Organization. Plan to build affordable houses (able to floats on swamps) in the fourth largest population (245,000,000) in the world. They needs 10,000,000 affordable houses. Their land and their neighboring countries (The Philippines, Malaysia, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Bangladesh, India, Pakistan) are sinking. I could help to build floating houses with Biofoam. But last week, I start learning about Biochar and today I studied your Graphitic Carbon Foam. I am not sure, but I know Indonesia has plenty of Fly ash from Sugar Cane refinery, Biochar from Rice Husk, Recycle Misc. Wood, Coconut and Palm Oil wastes. My expertise is in Architecture: Light Weight Ready to Assemble Building Component System. I have basic ideas about charcoal batteries, natural salt water distillation, heating and storage, passive energy natural day lights, DC battery powered LED, heating, conditioning, refrigerating and air circulating. I am sure this is your field, let’s talk and help people in needs. samueltanh@gmail.com.

author: @octavstanculescu1100
text: I think that the patent system was put in place to stimulate people to invent. But now it has gone too far and it has the exact oposite effect. In order to develop something new, most of the time you have to build on something that is already under patent and some really good ideas are left unexplored. But I think a change is nearer than you think. I think facebook and youtube have shown that notriety is more important than money and can even be converted to money trough advertising. And the music industry is already turning towards free distribution. The movie industry will probably folow and at some point even the technologies would probably be made freely available all in exchange for fame and notoriety. Even the inventors will see at some point that beeing famous is better than having lots of money and will turn away from the patent system.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: You make some really good points and I agree

author: @Patrik_76
text:
I believe in the open source principle that is split into 3 parts.

1. You can get all the info you need with the option of donations or a small fee for different services in the open source spirit. Parts of open source research cost money as we all know, so, the fee is only a return to them who spend time and resources for you.

2. You attend a workshop, physically or from a distance. Here, the cost from taking part of the workshop depend on many things. Sometimes it can be entirely free, but most of the time, money is involved for many reasons. Producing anything is always an investment in time, energy and resources. So even if you have everything you need, it probably was bought or payed for at an earlier stage.

3. You can purchase what is offered in the open source project if you cannot build or develop the product yourself with the option of levels in regards to quality, layout, size and so forth.

So in a system like this, all knowledge is free to do with as you please as long as you understand that you are responsible for your own actions and if you want a limited responsibility or do not have the time or desire to build or otherwise, you can pay someone ells to do the work for you.

I can relate to the topic being debated in this video, I was involved in some drama a few years back were work put in by me was going to be patented, but instead, I got ripped off and in that process lost the interest all together for patents and what like. Now ofc sometimes one must be cautious when new technology is being developed for not everybody is truly ready for the responsibility it might include, and that is really sad. Humans, most of us, still have a very long road ahead of us until we reach equal value as in the right to explore and live our life's free with the understanding of mutual respect and care and I like being part of that, the opportunity to help others to reach an understanding of what that means.

And as usual, thank you for your contribution, it is of great value.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like the points you raise - a lot - i think when one has these views so often you feel like you are the only one who sees the world this way - but i don't think that is true - i think there are far many more people out there who think the same way and i think that view is growing

replies.author: @Patrik_76
replies.text: Absolutely and getting and / or having a patent is often not beneficial for the general public and often reflect greed. It also often draw unwanted attention, so keeping things open source or in that nature often keeps the attention focus low. Spreading the knowledge is perhaps the greatest protection. Now ofc, this does not mean that you cannot profit from it or have a descent salary to live of, you can. Being wealthy is a perception and very difficult to define since its a personal view most of the time.

replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Indeed, more people are waking up, fed up with the same society built upon greed. Hopefully one day human energy will be hinged upon a resource based economy with a fair share for all living things. raping oceans and forest's for corporate greed is exactly what the patent system is all about supporting.

author: @jimjam623
text: The most lucid, succinct argument against the current patent system I've had the pleasure of viewing. Here's to the health and happiness of yourself - and the rest of society. Also, another vote of thanks for ALL your fascinating videos. Keep up the wonderful work!

replies.author: @RichRap3D
replies.text:
Another excellent video and sound point of view, keep talking sense, share with the good and ignore the parasites.

Around four years ago without realizing it, I made a similar decision to start sharing things I was working on with anyone and everyone. Whilst the 'reward' (not that I was looking for one) has not been financial, it has changed my life and been one of the best things I have done to date in a 20+ year career.

I'm not anti-patent, just very disappointed in the ability for a patent to actually mean anything for a small to medium sized company or individual.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: actually same here - i think patents in principle are great - it's just they have been corrupted

author: @MathewTitus
text: That's a great way of looking at things Robert. I was one of those people who have had the bitter experience of having my ideas stolen from me while in University - in my early 20's. My plan WAS to make millions and millions from a patent - and perhaps end up surrounded by grass skirts in Tahiti - before the age of 30. That never really worked out - so here I am in my 40's - still looking for great ideas, but no longer bothering if they are marketable. No grass skirts and the end of this rainbow

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think having your ideas stolen is quite a common story mate and the patent system and shameless people not only allow this but encourage it. Still - at the end of the day I think it is more important to feel you have done something and gained the respect of your peers - far more important than money
